questions.text: Which of the following statements about derived units is correct?

options.A: Derived units are independent of fundamental units.
options.B: Derived units are formed by combining fundamental units.
options.C: Derived units are used only in theoretical physics.
options.D: Derived units have no relation with physical quantities.

questions.explanation: Derived units are obtained by combining fundamental units through multiplication or division based on the physical quantity being measured. For example, velocity is measured in metre per second, which is formed from the fundamental units metre and second.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is not correct regarding the unit of force?

options.C: It is obtained from mass and acceleration

questions.explanation: Force is not a fundamental quantity; it is derived from mass and acceleration using the relation F = ma. Its SI unit is newton, which is defined as the force required to produce an acceleration of one metre per second squared in a mass of one kilogram. Hence, calling it a fundamental unit is incorrect.

questions.text: Which one of the following pairs contains only scalar quantities?
questions.explanation: Speed and distance are both scalar quantities because they are described only by magnitude and not by direction. Velocity, force, displacement, and acceleration are vector quantities since direction is essential in each case.

questions.text: Which of the following situations correctly represents zero velocity but non-zero speed?

options.A: A body moving with constant acceleration in a straight line
options.B: A body completing one full round on a circular track
options.C: A body at rest on the ground
options.D: A body moving in a straight line with constant speed

questions.explanation: When a body completes one full revolution in a circular path, it returns to its initial position, so the displacement becomes zero. Since velocity depends on displacement, the velocity is zero. However, the body has travelled a non-zero distance, so its speed is non-zero.

questions.text: Which of the following statements correctly explains the motion of a stone tied to a string and whirled in a circle?

options.A: The string provides an outward force that keeps the stone moving.
options.B: The string provides a force towards the centre that keeps the stone in circular motion.
options.C: The stone moves in a circle without any force acting on it.
options.D: The force acts only when the stone changes its speed.

questions.explanation: When a stone is tied to a string and rotated, the string exerts a force towards the centre of the circular path. This force is the centripetal force, and it is responsible for continuously changing the direction of the stone's velocity. Without this inward force, the stone would move in a straight line.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is correct about centripetal force in uniform circular motion?

options.A: It changes both speed and direction of the body.
options.B: It changes only the speed of the body.
options.C: It changes only the direction of the body's velocity.
options.D: It has no effect on the motion of the body.

questions.explanation: In uniform circular motion, the speed of the body remains constant, but its direction keeps changing. This continuous change in direction is caused by centripetal force, which acts towards the centre of the circle. Since the magnitude of velocity remains unchanged, the force does not affect speed. Its role is solely to alter the direction of motion.

questions.text:
Assertion (A): Passengers in a moving bus are thrown forward when the driver suddenly applies brakes.
Reason (R): The passengers' bodies tend to continue in their state of motion due to inertia.

options.A: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.B: Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.C: A is true, but R is false
options.D: A is false, but R is true

questions.explanation: Newton's first law states that a body resists any change in its existing state of rest or motion. This property is called inertia. When the bus is moving, the passengers' bodies are also in motion. If the driver applies brakes suddenly, the lower part of the body slows down with the bus, but the upper part tends to keep moving forward. That is why the passenger jerks forward. So, the reason correctly explains the assertion.

questions.text: A person carries a stone from the Earth to the Moon. What happens to its mass and weight?

options.B: Mass remains the same, but weight decreases
options.C: Mass decreases, but weight remains the same
options.D: Both mass and weight remain the same

questions.explanation: Mass is an intrinsic property of matter and does not depend on location. If a body is taken from the Earth to the Moon, the amount of matter in it remains unchanged, so its mass stays the same. Weight depends on gravitational acceleration, and the Moon's gravitational pull is much weaker than that of the Earth. Therefore, the same object weighs less on the Moon.

questions.text: During free fall near the surface of the Earth, the acceleration of a body is:

options.C: equal to acceleration due to gravity
options.D: different for heavy and light bodies

questions.explanation: A freely falling body near the Earth's surface moves with acceleration due to gravity, denoted by g. This acceleration has the same value for all bodies in the absence of air resistance. It does not depend on the mass of the falling object. That is why a heavy body and a light body both fall with the same acceleration in ideal conditions.

questions.text: Which of the following statements correctly explains why the soles of shoes and vehicle tyres are made rough?

options.A: Rough surfaces reduce all kinds of friction.
options.B: Rough surfaces increase friction and improve grip on the ground.
options.C: Rough surfaces make the body lighter during motion.
options.D: Rough surfaces are used only for decorative appearance.

questions.explanation: The soles of shoes and the surfaces of tyres are made rough to increase friction with the ground. Greater friction provides better grip and reduces the chance of slipping, especially while walking, running, or stopping a vehicle. If the surface were too smooth, the contact would offer less resistance to slipping.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describes fluid friction?

options.A: It acts only between solid surfaces
options.B: It acts when a body moves through a liquid or gas
options.C: It is independent of the speed of the body
options.D: It is always greater than static friction

questions.explanation: Fluid friction, also known as drag, acts on objects moving through fluids such as liquids and gases. It arises due to the resistance offered by the fluid to the motion of the body. The magnitude of this friction depends on factors like the speed of the object, the nature of the fluid, and the shape of the body.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is correct about the Kelvin scale?

options.A: It has negative temperatures in common use
options.B: Its zero is based on the freezing point of water

questions.explanation: The Kelvin scale is an absolute temperature scale, and its zero point is absolute zero, the lowest possible temperature in theory. At this temperature, molecular motion is minimum. Unlike Celsius and Fahrenheit scales, Kelvin does not use the degree symbol. Its unit is simply kelvin, written as K. The freezing point of water on this scale is 273 K, not zero.

questions.text: Heat conduction is the process of transfer of heat:

options.A: by actual movement of particles from one place to another
options.B: from a hotter region to a colder region without bulk movement of matter

questions.explanation: Conduction is the transfer of heat from a region of higher temperature to a region of lower temperature without any actual bulk movement of the substance. In this process, particles do not travel from one place to another; instead, they transfer energy to neighboring particles through collisions and vibrations. Conduction is most effective in solids, especially metals.

questions.text: Why does the handle of a metal spoon become hot when its other end is kept in hot tea?

options.A: Because hot tea rises upward through the spoon
options.B: Because heat travels through the metal by conduction
options.C: Because the spoon absorbs heat from the surroundings
options.D: Because heat travels through the metal by convection

questions.explanation: Metals are good conductors of heat, so thermal energy passes through them easily. When one end of a metal spoon is placed in hot tea, the particles at that end gain energy and transfer it to neighboring particles. This process continues along the length of the spoon until the handle also becomes hot. There is no upward flow of tea through the spoon, and the effect is not due to convection.

questions.text: A person can walk more easily on dry sand if he uses wide-soled shoes because:

options.B: wide soles decrease pressure on the sand
options.C: wide soles increase the force exerted on the ground

questions.explanation: Pressure is inversely related to area when force remains the same. A person's weight acts as the force on the sand. When wide-soled shoes are used, the area of contact increases, so the pressure on the sand decreases. Because of this, the shoes sink less and walking becomes easier. The improvement is not due to reduced weight or zero friction.

questions.text: In a liquid at rest, pressure at a point depends mainly on:

options.A: the colour of the liquid and the shape of the vessel
options.B: the depth of the point below the liquid surface
options.C: the width of the container only
options.D: the surface area of the liquid only

questions.explanation: In a liquid at rest, pressure increases with depth because the liquid above exerts weight on the layers below. Therefore, a point deeper inside the liquid experiences greater pressure. This pressure depends on depth, density of the liquid, and gravity, but not on the shape or width of the vessel alone.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is correct?

options.A: Pitch depends on amplitude of sound
options.B: Pitch and loudness always increase together
options.C: Pitch depends on frequency, while loudness depends on amplitude
options.D: Pitch depends on the colour of the source

questions.explanation: Pitch and loudness are two different characteristics of sound. Pitch is determined by frequency and tells whether the sound is high or low. Loudness is determined mainly by amplitude and indicates whether the sound is soft or loud. A sound may have high pitch but low loudness, or low pitch but high loudness, depending on the source.

questions.text: When an object is placed between the pole and the focus of a concave mirror, the image formed is:
questions.explanation: When an object is placed between the pole and the focus of a concave mirror, the reflected rays diverge, and their extensions appear to meet behind the mirror. As a result, the image formed is virtual and erect. Since the object is very close to the mirror, the image also appears enlarged. This is why concave mirrors are used as shaving mirrors and by dentists.

questions.text: Refraction of light occurs when light:

options.A: is reflected from a polished surface
options.B: passes from one medium to another medium
options.D: is absorbed completely by a black surface

questions.explanation: Refraction is the bending of light when it passes obliquely from one transparent medium to another. This bending takes place because the speed of light changes when it enters a medium of different optical density. If the light enters normally, bending may not be noticed, but the speed still changes. Reflection, absorption, and travel in vacuum are different optical phenomena.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is correct about optical density and refractive index?

options.A: A medium with lower refractive index is optically denser
options.B: Optical density and refractive index are unrelated
options.C: A medium with higher refractive index is optically denser
options.D: Optical density depends only on colour of the medium

questions.explanation: Optical density describes how much a medium slows down light. A medium in which light slows down more is called optically denser. Since refractive index increases when the speed of light in the medium decreases, a higher refractive index indicates greater optical density. This is why glass is optically denser than air.

questions.text:
Assertion (A): Ohm's law is not valid if the temperature of the conductor changes significantly.
Reason (R): Resistance of a conductor may change with temperature.

options.A: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.B: Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.C: A is true, but R is false
options.D: A is false, but R is true

questions.explanation: Ohm's law holds only when the physical conditions of the conductor, especially temperature, remain constant. If the temperature changes, the resistance of the conductor may also change. Once resistance is no longer constant, the direct proportionality between potential difference and current does not remain exact. That is why Ohm's law cannot be applied strictly under changing thermal conditions. The assertion is correct, and the reason gives the exact basis for that limitation.

questions.text: A student notices that when too many electrical appliances are switched on at the same time, the fuse wire melts and the circuit breaks. What is the most appropriate explanation?

options.A: The fuse melts because voltage becomes zero in the circuit
options.B: The fuse melts because excessive current produces too much heat in it
options.C: The fuse melts because the appliances stop using electricity
options.D: The fuse melts because the neutral wire becomes shorter

questions.explanation: A fuse is a safety device made of a wire with low melting point. When excessive current flows through the circuit, the fuse wire gets heated strongly due to the heating effect of electric current. If the current exceeds the safe limit, the heat produced becomes enough to melt the fuse wire and break the circuit. This protects the appliances and wiring from damage.

questions.text: In a house, a short circuit suddenly occurs and sparks are seen near a damaged wire. Which statement best explains the situation?

options.A: A short circuit happens when current becomes too small to flow
options.B: A short circuit happens when live and neutral wires come into direct contact, causing a large current
options.C: A short circuit happens because the fuse stops working normally
options.D: A short circuit happens only when an appliance is switched off

questions.explanation: A short circuit occurs when the live wire and neutral wire come into direct contact due to damaged insulation or faulty wiring. This creates a path of very low resistance, so an abnormally large current flows through the circuit. The sudden rise in current produces intense heating and may cause sparks or fire. In such situations, the fuse is expected to melt and break the circuit.

questions.text: Which of the following statements about magnetic poles is correct?

options.C: Like poles repel each other and unlike poles attract each other
options.D: Magnetic poles have no interaction with each other

questions.explanation: Magnetic poles always exist in pairs, called north and south poles. When two magnets are brought close to each other, like poles such as north–north or south–south repel each other, while unlike poles such as north–south attract each other. This interaction is a fundamental property of magnetism and is consistently observed.

questions.text: Which of the following devices works on the principle of magnetism?
questions.explanation: An electric motor works on the interaction between magnetic fields and electric current. When current passes through a coil placed in a magnetic field, forces act on the coil and produce rotation. This converts electrical energy into mechanical energy. Devices like thermometers and barometers work on thermal and pressure principles respectively, not magnetism.

questions.text: What happens to the velocity of a body while it is falling freely under gravity, neglecting air resistance?

options.A: It remains constant throughout the motion
options.D: It first increases and then becomes zero

questions.explanation: In free fall, the body experiences a constant acceleration due to gravity. A constant acceleration produces a uniform change in velocity with time. Since gravity acts downward throughout the motion, the speed of the falling body keeps increasing at a steady rate as long as air resistance is neglected. This means the velocity does not remain constant or decrease. It increases uniformly with time because the body is continuously accelerated by the Earth's gravitational pull.

questions.text: Which one of the following statements correctly distinguishes mass from weight?

options.A: Mass changes from place to place, while weight remains constant
options.B: Mass is a force, while weight is the quantity of matter in a body
options.C: Mass is the quantity of matter in a body, while weight is the gravitational force acting on it
options.D: Mass and weight are both measured in newton

questions.explanation: Mass is the amount of matter present in a body, so it remains the same regardless of location. Weight, on the other hand, is the force with which the Earth or any other celestial body pulls that mass toward itself. Since weight depends on gravitational acceleration, it can change from place to place. On the Moon, for example, the same body has the same mass but a smaller weight. Thus mass is constant, while weight depends on gravity.

questions.text: A swimmer is able to move forward in water because:

options.A: water pulls the swimmer forward without any reaction
options.B: the swimmer pushes water backward, and water pushes the swimmer forward
options.C: the swimmer moves forward because of the frictional force in water
options.D: the swimmer becomes lighter inside water, which makes him swim

questions.explanation: A swimmer moves forward by pushing water backward with hands and feet. Water then exerts an equal and opposite force on the swimmer, pushing the body forward. The forward motion is therefore produced by the reaction force of water. This is a practical application of Newton's third law, where action and reaction act on different bodies.

questions.text:
Assertion (A): If the same force acts on two bodies of different masses, the lighter body will have greater acceleration.
Reason (R): According to Newton's second law, acceleration is inversely proportional to mass for a given force.

options.A: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.B: Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.C: A is true, but R is false
options.D: A is false, but R is true

questions.explanation: Newton's second law shows that acceleration produced in a body depends on the applied force and the mass of the body. For the same force, acceleration is given by a = F/m. This means that when mass decreases, acceleration increases. Therefore, if equal force is applied to two bodies, the lighter body will respond more quickly and gain greater acceleration. The heavier body will accelerate less under the same force. Hence both statements are true, and the reason properly explains the assertion.

questions.text: Which of the following statements about centripetal force is correct?

options.A: It acts along the direction of motion of the body.
options.B: It acts away from the centre of the circular path.
options.C: It acts towards the centre of the circular path.
options.D: It acts only when the speed of the body changes.

questions.explanation: In circular motion, a body continuously changes its direction, even if its speed remains constant. This change in direction requires a force directed towards the centre of the circle, known as centripetal force. It is always perpendicular to the velocity of the body and keeps it in circular motion. It does not act along the direction of motion and is not directed outward. Its presence is essential even when speed is constant.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is correct about the unit of power?

options.A: It is a fundamental unit measured in joule
options.B: It is a derived unit measured in watt
options.C: It is equal to kilogram per metre
options.D: It does not depend on time

questions.explanation: Power is defined as the rate at which work is done or energy is transferred. Since work is measured in joule and time in seconds, power is expressed as joule per second, which is called watt. This clearly shows that power depends on both energy and time, making it a derived quantity. It is not a fundamental unit, and it cannot be expressed in unrelated forms like kilogram per metre.

questions.text: Which of the following statements best explains why passengers in a bus tend to fall backward when the bus starts suddenly?

options.A: The passengers are pushed backward by the seat due to friction alone.
options.B: The upper part of the body tends to remain at rest because of inertia, while the lower part moves with the bus.
options.C: The bus moves faster than the force of gravity.
options.D: The passengers fall backward because their weight suddenly increases.

questions.explanation: When a bus starts suddenly, the lower part of the passenger's body moves forward with the bus because it is in contact with the seat or floor. But the upper part tends to remain in its previous state of rest due to inertia. This creates a backward jerk. Newton's first law explains this behavior clearly: a body resists any change in its state of rest or motion unless acted upon by an external force.

questions.text: Which of the following statements correctly reflects the role of external force in Newton's first law?

options.A: External force is needed to maintain motion in all cases.
options.B: External force is needed only to start motion, not to stop it.
options.C: External force is needed to change the state of rest or uniform straight-line motion of a body.
options.D: External force is needed only when the body is at rest.

questions.explanation: According to Newton's first law, force is not required to maintain rest or uniform straight-line motion. It is required only when the existing state has to be changed. A body may be at rest or moving uniformly; in both cases, an external unbalanced force is needed to alter that state. This is why option (C) is correct. The other options wrongly treat force as something needed for motion itself.

questions.text: A body is said to be in free fall when:

options.A: it moves upward with constant speed
options.B: only gravitational force acts on it
options.C: it falls in air with no motion
options.D: no force acts on it at all

questions.explanation: A body is in free fall when gravity alone acts on it. In such motion, no other force such as air resistance, friction, or applied force is considered. The body then falls under the influence of the Earth's gravitational pull and acquires acceleration due to gravity. Free fall does not mean absence of force; it means the only force present is gravitational force.

questions.text: Potential energy of a body is the energy possessed by it due to:
questions.explanation: Potential energy is the energy stored in a body because of its position or arrangement. A body raised above the ground has gravitational potential energy because of its height. A stretched spring has elastic potential energy because of its changed shape. This form of energy is not due to motion, so it is different from kinetic energy. It depends on the condition of the body in a force field or on its configuration relative to other bodies.

questions.text: Which of the following statements best explains why it is more difficult to walk on a sandy road than on a cemented road?

options.A: Sand always produces more friction than a hard road.
options.B: The foot gets less firm backward push from sand, so proper frictional support is reduced.
options.C: Sand reduces the weight of the body while walking.
options.D: Walking on sand becomes difficult because air resistance increases near the ground.

questions.explanation: Walking requires friction between the foot and the ground. When we walk, we push the ground backward, and the ground pushes us forward through friction. On a sandy road, the sand shifts backward instead of giving a firm reaction, so effective frictional support becomes less useful for forward motion. That is why walking feels harder. The difficulty is not due to reduced body weight or air resistance, but due to poor support from the loose surface.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is correct regarding sliding friction?

options.A: It is always greater than static friction
options.B: It acts when a body slides over another surface
options.C: It acts only in liquids and gases
options.D: It is equal to rolling friction

questions.explanation: Sliding friction comes into play when one surface moves over another with actual relative motion between them. It is generally less than the maximum static friction because once motion begins, the irregularities between surfaces do not interlock as strongly. It does not act in fluids, as fluid friction is a separate type. Rolling friction is also different and usually smaller than sliding friction.

questions.text: The value of 50° C on the Fahrenheit scale is:
questions.explanation: To convert Celsius into Fahrenheit, the relation F = [(9/5)C + 32] is used. Substituting C = 50, the result becomes F = 122° F.

questions.text: If the frequency of a vibrating source is increased, the sound produced becomes:
questions.explanation: When the frequency of vibration increases, the number of vibrations produced per second also increases. As a result, the sound is heard as more shrill, which means its pitch becomes higher. Shrillness is directly related to frequency. Loudness depends mainly on amplitude, not frequency. The speed of sound depends on the medium through which it travels and not on the frequency of the vibrating source.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is correct about the size of the image formed by a plane mirror?

options.A: It is always larger than the object
options.B: It is always smaller than the object
options.C: It is always equal to the size of the object
options.D: It depends on the colour of the object

questions.explanation: A plane mirror always forms an image that is of the same size as the object. The magnification produced by a plane mirror is unity, meaning the image height is equal to the object height. This is a fundamental property of plane mirrors, resulting from the law of reflection. The size of the image does not depend on the distance of the object from the mirror or its colour.

questions.text: A pencil partly dipped in water appears bent at the water surface because of:
questions.explanation: The pencil appears bent because light coming from the submerged part travels from water into air and gets refracted at the surface. As the light changes medium, its speed changes and the rays bend away from the normal. The eye traces these bent rays backward in a straight line, so the underwater part appears displaced from its actual position. This apparent change in position creates the bent appearance. The effect is due to refraction, not reflection, dispersion, or interference.

questions.text: Which of the following statements about refractive index is correct?

options.A: It tells how much heat a medium can absorb
options.B: It tells how much a medium slows down light compared to vacuum
options.C: It is equal to the wavelength of light in air
options.D: It remains zero for all transparent media

questions.explanation: Refractive index expresses how light behaves in a medium in comparison with its speed in vacuum. It is defined as the ratio of the speed of light in vacuum to its speed in the given medium. If light travels more slowly in a medium, the refractive index is higher. This is why optically denser media have greater refractive index. It is not related to heat absorption or wavelength alone, and it is never zero for a transparent medium.

questions.text:
Assertion (A): If the current through a conductor becomes zero, the resistance of the conductor must also become zero.
Reason (R): According to Ohm's law, resistance is given by the ratio V/I.

options.A: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
options.B: Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A
options.C: A is true, but R is false
options.D: A is false, but R is true

questions.explanation: The assertion is false because zero current does not mean zero resistance. Current may become zero simply because no potential difference is applied or the circuit is open. Resistance is a property of the conductor and does not vanish just because current stops. The reason is true in the sense that resistance is expressed by the ratio V/I under Ohm's law, but this relation must be interpreted within proper circuit conditions. Therefore, the assertion is false while the reason is true.

questions.text: A person touches the metal body of an electric iron that has developed a fault, but does not receive a shock because the appliance is properly earthed. What does earthing do in this case?

options.A: It increases the voltage of the appliance so that shock is avoided
options.B: It provides a low-resistance path for leakage current to flow into the ground
options.C: It stops the appliance from using electric current
options.D: It converts alternating current into direct current

questions.explanation: Earthing is a protective arrangement in which the metal body of an appliance is connected to the Earth through a low-resistance wire. If a fault causes the live wire to touch the metal body, the leakage current flows safely into the ground instead of passing through the human body. This reduces the risk of electric shock. Earthing does not increase voltage, stop the appliance from consuming electricity, or convert one type of current into another.

questions.text: Which of the following materials is strongly attracted by a magnet?
questions.explanation: Materials that are strongly attracted by a magnet are called ferromagnetic substances. Iron is a typical ferromagnetic material because its internal magnetic domains can easily align in the presence of a magnetic field, producing a strong attraction. Copper and aluminium are not strongly attracted; aluminium shows very weak attraction, while copper is practically non-magnetic. Wood is non-magnetic. The strong attraction of iron is due to its internal structure, not simply its weight or appearance.

questions.text:
Which of the following statements about kinetic energy is incorrect?
1. It is the energy possessed by a body due to its position.
2. It is independent of the speed of the body.
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy is the energy possessed by a body because of its motion. Whenever a body moves, it is capable of doing work due to that motion, and this ability is measured as kinetic energy. A stationary body has no kinetic energy. This energy depends on both mass and speed, and its value is given by (1/2)mv². Statement 1 is incorrect because it describes potential energy, not kinetic energy. Statement 2 is incorrect because kinetic energy is directly proportional to the square of the speed.

questions.text:
Which of the following statements are correct?
1. A body in uniform motion covers unequal distances in equal intervals of time
2. For a body in uniform motion, distance-time graph is a straight line.
questions.explanation: Uniform motion means that a body covers equal distances in equal intervals of time. This shows that the speed of the body remains constant throughout the motion. If the distances covered in equal time intervals are different, the motion is not uniform. Statement 1 is incorrect because it describes non-uniform motion. Statement 2 is correct because in uniform motion, distance increases by equal amounts in equal intervals of time, producing a straight-line distance-time graph with constant slope.

questions.text:
Which of the following statements are correct?
1. The speed of a body in uniform motion increases continuously with time.
2. The distance-time graph of uniform motion is always parallel to the time axis.
questions.explanation: The defining feature of uniform motion is constant speed. This means the body moves with the same rate of covering distance throughout the motion. Since the speed does not change, the body travels equal distances in equal intervals of time. If speed were increasing or decreasing, the motion would become non-uniform. Statement 1 is incorrect because speed in uniform motion is constant. Statement 2 is incorrect because the distance-time graph of uniform motion is a straight line with a constant slope, not parallel to the time axis. A graph parallel to the time axis would indicate that the body is at rest.

questions.text:
Which of the following statements are correct regarding escape velocity from the Earth?
1. It is independent of the mass of the projected body
2. It has the same value regardless of the direction of projection, if air resistance is ignored
questions.explanation: Escape velocity depends on the gravitational field of the Earth, which is determined by the Earth's mass and radius. It does not depend on the mass of the object being projected. A light object and a heavy object both require the same escape velocity from the same point, if air resistance is neglected. The direction of projection does not change its required value in this ideal treatment. Therefore, both statements are correct.

questions.text: Which of the following statements correctly describes the effect of doubling the speed of a body on its kinetic energy?

options.B: The kinetic energy becomes four times

questions.explanation: Kinetic energy depends on the square of speed, not simply on speed itself. According to the expression (1/2)mv², if the speed of a body becomes twice its original value, the square of speed becomes four times. As a result, the kinetic energy also becomes four times its initial value, provided the mass remains unchanged. This square dependence is very important because it shows that kinetic energy rises rapidly with increase in speed.

questions.text: When a magnesium ribbon is burnt in air, it produces a coloured flame.
questions.explanation: When a magnesium ribbon is burnt in air, it burns with a dazzling white flame and changes into a white powder. This powder is magnesium oxide. It is formed due to the reaction between magnesium and oxygen present in the air. 2Mg + O₂ → 2MgO (white powder).

questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
I. Litmus solution is a colourless liquid, which is extracted from lichen.
II. A stain of curry on a white cloth becomes reddish-brown when soap, which is acidic in nature, is scrubbed on it.
Identify the correct statement/s.
questions.explanation: Litmus solution is a natural dye extracted from lichens, but it is not colourless. In its neutral state, it is typically a purple or mauve color, which turns red in acidic solutions and blue in alkaline solutions. Therefore, Statement I is incorrect. A stain of curry (containing turmeric) on a white cloth turns reddish-brown when soap is applied. However, this occurs because soap is basic in nature, not acidic. The basic nature of the soap reacts with the turmeric, acting as an indicator. Therefore, Statement II is incorrect.

questions.text:
Consider the following statements.
I. The ability of metals to be drawn into thin wires is called ductility.
II. The metals are good conductors of heat and thus have low melting points.
III. The metals that produce a sound on striking a hard surface are said to be sonorous.
Identify the correct statement/s.
questions.explanation: Ductility is defined as the ability of a material (especially metals) to be stretched or drawn into thin wires without breaking. Gold is the most ductile metal. A wire of about 2 km length can be drawn from one gram of gold. Hence Statement I is correct. Sonorous refers to the property of metals to produce a ringing sound when they strike a hard surface. That is why school bells are made of metals. Hence Statement III is correct. While metals are indeed good conductors of heat, they generally have high melting points. For example, iron and copper require extreme temperatures to melt, which is why they are used in high-heat applications like cookware and engines. The best conductors of heat are silver and copper. Lead and mercury are comparatively poor conductors of heat. Hence Statement II is incorrect.

questions.text:
Fill in the blanks.
In _____, each carbon atom is bonded to three other carbon atoms in the same plane giving a hexagonal array. One of these bonds is a double-bond, and thus the valency of carbon is satisfied.
questions.explanation: The element carbon occurs in different forms in nature with widely varying physical properties. Both diamond and graphite are formed by carbon atoms, the difference lies in the manner in which the carbon atoms are bonded to one another. Diamond is the hardest substance known while graphite is smooth and slippery. Graphite is also a very good conductor of electricity. In diamond, each carbon atom is bonded to four other carbon atoms forming a rigid three-dimensional structure. In graphite, each carbon atom is bonded to three other carbon atoms in the same plane giving a hexagonal array. One of these bonds is a double-bond, and thus the valency of carbon is satisfied. Graphite structure is formed by the hexagonal arrays being placed in layers one above the other. Fullerenes form another class of carbon allotropes. The first one to be identified was C-60 which has carbon atoms arranged in the shape of a football.

questions.text: Esters are most commonly formed by reaction of
questions.explanation: Esters are most commonly formed by reaction of an acid and an alcohol. Ethanoic acid reacts with absolute ethanol in the presence of an acid catalyst to give an ester. Generally, esters are sweet-smelling substances. These are used in making perfumes and as flavouring agents. On treating with sodium hydroxide, which is an alkali, the ester is converted back to alcohol and sodium salt of carboxylic acid. This reaction is known as saponification because it is used in the preparation of soap. Soaps are sodium or potassium salts of long chain carboxylic acid.

questions.text:
Consider the statements.
I. Saturated hydrocarbons will generally give a clean flame while burning.
II. Unsaturated carbon compounds will give a yellow flame with lots of black smoke.
Identify the correct statement/s.
questions.explanation: Saturated hydrocarbons (like alkanes) contain only single carbon-carbon bonds and typically have a lower carbon-to-hydrogen ratio. This allows for complete combustion in the presence of sufficient air, resulting in a clean, blue flame with no soot. Hence Statement I is correct. Unsaturated carbon compounds (like alkenes and alkynes) contain double or triple bonds and have a higher percentage of carbon. This higher carbon content often leads to incomplete combustion in air, causing unburnt carbon particles (soot) to glow and produce a yellow, smoky flame. Hence Statement II is correct.

questions.text: A series of compounds in which the same functional group substitutes for hydrogen in a carbon chain is called
questions.explanation: A homologous series is a sequence of organic compounds that share the same functional group and a similar general formula. In such a series, successive members differ from one another by a specific repeating unit, typically a -CH₂ (methylene) group. Because they possess the same functional group, all members of the series exhibit very similar chemical properties, though their physical properties—such as boiling and melting points—change gradually as the carbon chain length increases.

questions.text: The process of forming a thick oxide layer of Aluminium, to make it corrosion-resistant is known as
questions.explanation: Anodizing is an electro-chemical process that artificially increases the thickness of the natural oxide layer on the surface of Aluminium and its alloys. The Aluminium part acts as the anode in an electrolytic cell containing an acid (usually dilute sulphuric acid). When current passes through, oxygen ions react with the Aluminium surface to form a dense, durable, and corrosion-resistant layer of Aluminium oxide (Al₂O₃). This thickened layer significantly improves corrosion resistance, wear resistance, and provides a porous surface that can be dyed for decorative finishes.

questions.text: When Silver articles is exposed to air, it becomes black after some time. This is because a thin layer of ______ is formed.
questions.explanation: When silver articles are exposed to air, they react with traces of hydrogen sulphide (H₂S) gas present in the atmosphere. This chemical reaction results in the formation of a thin, black layer of silver sulphide (Ag₂S) on the surface of the metal. This phenomenon is commonly known as tarnishing or corrosion of silver.

questions.text:
Consider the following statements.
I. The sulphide ores are converted into oxides by heating strongly in the presence of excess air. This process is known as roasting.
II. The nitrate ores are changed into oxides by heating strongly in limited air. This process is known as calcination.
Identify the correct statement/s.
questions.explanation: Statement I is correct - Roasting is a metallurgical process where sulphide ores are heated strongly at a temperature below their melting point in the presence of excess air to convert them into metal oxides. Statement II is incorrect - Calcination is the process of heating carbonate or hydrated ores (not nitrate ores) strongly in the absence or a limited supply of air to convert them into oxides. Nitrate ores are generally decomposed by heating but are not the primary target of the calcination process in the context of standard ore refining.

questions.text: A visually challenged student has to perform a lab test to detect the presence of acid in a given solution. What will be the acid-base indicator preferred by him?
questions.explanation: A visually challenged student will prefer an olfactory indicator, such as vanilla essence, clove oil, or onion extract. These substances change their odour (or lose it) in acidic or basic solutions, allowing detection through smell rather than visual colour changes. Clove oil is an olfactory indicator. These are the substances that give one type of odour in an acidic medium so a visually challenged student prefers to use clove oil as an acid-base indicator. As in basic solutions, the smell of clove oil disappears while the smell is retained when mixed with an acid.

questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
Statement I: Hydrogen gas is not evolved when a metal reacts with nitric acid.
Statement II: HNO₃ is a strong oxidising agent.

options.A: Both the statements are individually true and statement II is the correct explanation of Statement I.
options.B: Both the statements are individually true but Statement II is not the correct explanation of Statement I.
options.C: Statement I is true, but Statement II is false.
options.D: Statement I is false, but Statement II is true.

questions.explanation: Generally, when metals react with acids, hydrogen gas is evolved. However, nitric acid (HNO₃) is an exception because it is a very strong oxidizing agent. So, it oxidizes the hydrogen (H₂) produced during the reaction to water (H₂O) and gets reduced itself to nitrogen oxides. Therefore, Both the statements are true and statement II is the correct explanation of Statement I.

questions.text:
Consider the following chemical reaction: Al₂O₃ + X HCl → Y AlCl₃ + Z H₂O
Which of the following will be the values of the coefficients X, Y, and Z respectively?
questions.explanation: The balanced chemical equation is: Al₂O₃ + 6HCl → 2AlCl₃ + 3H₂O. Therefore, the values for the coefficients are X = 6, Y = 2, Z = 3.

questions.text:
Match List-I (Natural Source) with List-II (Acid) and select the correct answer using the codes given below:
List I (Natural source)
I. Vinegar
II. Tamarind
III. Lemon
IV. Nettle sting
List II (Acid)
A. Tartaric acid
B. Methanoic acid
C. Acetic acid
D. Citric acid
questions.explanation: The natural sources and their corresponding acids are as follows: Vinegar - Acetic acid. Tamarind - Tartaric acid. Lemon - Citric acid. Nettle sting - Methanoic acid (also known as formic acid).

questions.text: Which of the following is the correct arrangement of the given metals in the order of their reactivity?
questions.explanation: Metals are arranged in the reactivity series based on their tendency to lose electrons and form positive ions. Sodium is an alkali metal (Group 1) and is highly reactive, reacting vigorously even with cold water. Magnesium (Group 2) is less reactive than sodium and typically requires hot water for a faster reaction. Zinc and iron are transition metals and are even less reactive, generally requiring steam to react. Zinc is more reactive than iron because it is more electropositive. The standard decreasing order of reactivity for these specific metals is: Sodium > Magnesium > Zinc > Iron.

questions.text: What happens to the particles of a solid when its temperature is increased?

options.B: Their kinetic energy increases and they vibrate faster.
options.C: Their kinetic energy decreases and they stop moving.

questions.explanation: On increasing the temperature of solids, the kinetic energy of the particles increases. Due to the increase in kinetic energy, the particles start vibrating with greater speed. The energy supplied by heat overcomes the forces of attraction between the particles. The particles leave their fixed positions and start moving more freely. A stage is reached when the solid melts and is converted to a liquid.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is correct about evaporation?

options.A: It occurs only at the boiling point.
options.B: It can occur at any temperature below the boiling point.
options.C: It requires the liquid to be heated under high pressure.

questions.explanation: Water, when left uncovered, slowly changes into vapour. Wet clothes dry up. We know that particles of matter are always moving and are never at rest. At a given temperature in any gas, liquid or solid, there are particles with different amounts of kinetic energy. In the case of liquids, a small fraction of particles at the surface, having higher kinetic energy, is able to break away from the forces of attraction of other particles and gets converted into vapour. This phenomenon of change of liquid into vapours at any temperature below its boiling point is called evaporation.

questions.text: Why is cotton preferred over synthetic clothes in summer?

options.B: Cotton absorbs sweat and allows easy evaporation.

questions.explanation: During summer, we perspire more because of the mechanism of our body which keeps us cool. We know that during evaporation, the particles at the surface of the liquid gain energy from the surroundings or body surface and change into vapour. The energy equal to the latent heat of vaporisation is absorbed from the body leaving the body cool. Cotton, being a good absorber of water helps in absorbing the sweat and exposing it to the atmosphere for easy evaporation.

questions.text: What is the key difference between a pure substance and a mixture?

options.A: Pure substances can be separated physically, mixtures cannot.
options.B: Mixtures contain more than one substance, pure substances have uniform composition.
options.C: Mixtures are always solids, pure substances are liquids.
options.D: Mixtures have chemical properties, pure substances do not.

questions.explanation: A pure substance consists of a single type of particles and has a uniform and definite composition throughout. In contrast, a mixture contains two or more substances (elements or compounds) mixed together, and its composition is not fixed.

questions.text: Why are alloys considered mixtures even though they cannot be separated physically?

options.B: Because they show the properties of their constituents and can have variable composition
options.D: Because they are made of pure substances only

questions.explanation: Alloys are mixtures of two or more metals or a metal and a non-metal and cannot be separated into their components by physical methods. But still, an alloy is considered as a mixture because it shows the properties of its constituents and can have variable composition. For example, brass is a mixture of approximately 30% zinc and 70% copper.

questions.text:
Statements:
1. A solution is a homogeneous mixture.
2. The solute particles in a solution are larger than 1 micrometer.
3. A solution scatters light strongly.
4. Solute particles in a solution can be separated by filtration.
Which statements are correct?
questions.explanation: A solution is a homogeneous mixture. The particles of a solution are smaller than 1 nm (10⁻⁹ metre) in diameter. So, they cannot be seen by naked eyes. Because of very small particle size, they do not scatter a beam of light passing through the solution. So, the path of light is not visible in a solution. The solute particles cannot be separated from the mixture by the process of filtration. The solute particles do not settle down when left undisturbed, that is, a solution is stable. Therefore, only statement 1 is correct.

questions.text: When sunlight enters a dark room through a small hole and the beam becomes visible, it is an example of:

options.B: Tyndall effect due to dust particles

questions.explanation: The particles of a colloid are uniformly spread throughout the solution. Due to the relatively smaller size of particles, as compared to that of a suspension, the mixture appears to be homogeneous. But actually, a colloidal solution is a heterogeneous mixture, for example, milk. Because of the small size of colloidal particles, we cannot see them with naked eyes. But, these particles can easily scatter a beam of visible light. This scattering of a beam of light is called the Tyndall effect after the name of the scientist who discovered this effect. Tyndall effect can also be observed when a fine beam of light enters a room through a small hole. This happens due to the scattering of light by the particles of dust and smoke in the air.

questions.text:
Assertion (A): Colloids cannot be separated by filtration.
Reason (R): Colloidal particles are very small in size.

options.A: Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
options.B: Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A.
options.C: A is true, but R is false.
options.D: A is false, but R is true.

questions.explanation: A colloid is a heterogeneous mixture. The size of particles of a colloid is too small to be individually seen with naked eyes. Colloids are big enough to scatter a beam of light passing through it and make its path visible. They do not settle down when left undisturbed, that is, a colloid is stable. They cannot be separated from the mixture by the process of filtration. But, a special technique of separation known as centrifugation can be used to separate the colloidal particles. The assertion and reason are both true, and the reason correctly explains why colloids cannot be separated by filtration.

questions.text: Change of state does not involve change in:
questions.explanation: The interconversion of states is a physical change because these changes occur without a change in composition and no change in the chemical nature of the substance. Although ice, water and water vapour all look different, they have the same chemical composition (H₂O). Therefore, change of state does not involve a change in composition.

questions.text: Who first used the term 'element' in 1661?
questions.explanation: Robert Boyle was the first scientist to use the term element in 1661. Antoine Laurent Lavoisier (1743–94), a French chemist, was the first to establish an experimentally useful definition of an element. He defined an element as a basic form of matter that cannot be broken down into simpler substances by chemical reactions.

questions.text:
Which of the following statements are correct?
1. Non-metals are usually not sonorous
2. Metals have lustre
3. Non-metals are good conductors
4. Metals can be drawn into wires
questions.explanation: Elements can be normally divided into metals, non-metals and metalloids. Metals usually show some or all of the following properties: They have a lustre (shine). They conduct heat and electricity. They are ductile (can be drawn into wires). They are malleable (can be hammered into thin sheets). They are sonorous (make a ringing sound when hit). Non-metals usually show some or all of the following properties: They display a variety of colours. They are poor conductors of heat and electricity. They are not lustrous, sonorous, or malleable. Therefore, statements 1, 2, and 4 are correct.

questions.text:
Which of the following are differences between mixtures and compounds?
1. Mixtures have variable composition
2. Compounds have fixed composition
3. Mixtures form new substances
4. Compounds have different properties than elements
questions.explanation: The differences between mixtures and compounds are: Elements or compounds just mix together to form a mixture and no new compound is formed (so 3 is incorrect). A mixture has a variable composition (1 correct). A mixture shows the properties of the constituent substances. The constituents can be separated fairly easily by physical methods. Elements react to form new compounds (so compounds have different properties than elements, 4 correct). The composition of each new substance is always fixed (2 correct). Therefore, 1, 2, and 4 are correct.

questions.text: Who stated the law of definite proportions?
questions.explanation: In a compound such as water, the ratio of the mass of hydrogen to the mass of oxygen is always 1:8, whatever the source of water. Thus, if 9 g of water is decomposed, 1 g of hydrogen and 8 g of oxygen are always obtained. This led to the law of constant proportions which is also known as the law of definite proportions. This law was stated by Proust as 'In a chemical substance the elements are always present in definite proportions by mass'.

questions.text:
Assertion (A): Atoms of different elements are identical.
Reason (R): They have different masses and chemical properties.

options.A: Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
options.B: Both A and R are true, but R is not the correct explanation of A.
options.C: A is true, but R is false.
options.D: A is false, but R is true.

questions.explanation: According to Dalton's atomic theory, all matter, whether an element, a compound or a mixture is composed of small particles called atoms. The postulates of this theory may be stated as follows: (i) All matter is made of very tiny particles called atoms, which participate in chemical reactions. (ii) Atoms are indivisible particles, which cannot be created or destroyed in a chemical reaction. (iii) Atoms of a given element are identical in mass and chemical properties. (iv) Atoms of different elements have different masses and chemical properties. Therefore, the assertion is false, but the reason is true.

questions.text: A group of atoms carrying a net charge is called a:
questions.explanation: Compounds composed of metals and nonmetals contain charged species. The charged species are known as ions. Ions may consist of a single charged atom or a group of atoms that have a net charge on them. An ion can be negatively or positively charged. A negatively charged ion is called an 'anion' and the positively charged ion, a 'cation'. A group of atoms carrying a charge is known as a polyatomic ion.

questions.text:
Which of the following statements about Thomson's atomic model are correct?
1. An atom consists of a positively charged sphere with electrons embedded in it.
2. The atom as a whole is electrically neutral because positive and negative charges are equal in magnitude.
questions.explanation: Thomson proposed the model of an atom to be similar to that of a Christmas pudding. The electrons, in a sphere of positive charge, were like currants (dry fruits) in a spherical Christmas pudding. We can also think of a watermelon, the positive charge in the atom is spread all over like the red edible part of the watermelon, while the electrons are studded in the positively charged sphere, like the seeds in the watermelon. Thomson proposed that: (i) An atom consists of a positively-charged sphere and the electrons are embedded in it. (ii) The negative and positive charges are equal in magnitude. So, the atom as a whole is electrically neutral. Therefore, both statements are correct.

questions.text: According to Bohr's model of the atom, electrons:

options.A: Can revolve in any orbit around the nucleus
options.C: Revolve in discrete orbits without radiating energy

questions.explanation: In order to overcome the objections raised against Rutherford's model of the atom, Neils Bohr put forward the following postulates about the model of an atom: (i) Only certain special orbits known as discrete orbits of electrons, are allowed inside the atom. (ii) While revolving in discrete orbits the electrons do not radiate energy.

questions.text: Why are certain isotopes of elements used in medicine and industry?

options.A: Because their chemical properties are different from the element
options.B: Because some isotopes have special physical or nuclear properties
options.C: Because isotopes are cheaper to produce
options.D: Because isotopes change the chemical reactions of elements

questions.explanation: Certain isotopes of elements are used in medicine and industry because they possess special physical or nuclear properties. For example, radioactive isotopes like Cobalt-60 are used in cancer treatment (radiotherapy), and Iodine-131 is used for thyroid diagnosis and treatment. These properties allow isotopes to be useful in various applications without changing the chemical behaviour of the element.

questions.text: Native minerals are:

options.A: Compounds made of two or more elements
options.B: Pure elements, either metals or non-metals

questions.explanation: Most rocks are a mixture of minerals, which can be viewed with the eyes, or by using a magnifying glass or a microscope. Some of the minerals are called native minerals, which are pure elements and not compounds. These can be metals, such as gold, silver, copper, etc., or non-metals like sulfur, carbon, etc. Most of the minerals are compounds made up of more than one element.

questions.text: Who proposed the Law of Octaves in 1865?
questions.explanation: The English chemist, John Alexander Newlands in 1865 propounded the Law of Octaves. He arranged the elements in increasing order of their atomic weights and noted that every eighth element had properties similar to the first element. The relationship was just like every eighth note that resembles the first in octaves of music. Newlands's Law of Octaves seemed to be true only for elements up to calcium. Although his idea was not widely accepted at that time, he, for his work, was later awarded Davy Medal in 1887 by the Royal Society, London.

questions.text: Proteins that act as biological catalysts in the body are called:
questions.explanation: Macromolecules of biological origin perform various functions in the body. For example, proteins which perform the role of biological catalysts in the body are called enzymes, those which are crucial to the communication system in the body are called receptors. Carrier proteins carry polar molecules across the cell membrane. Nucleic acids have coded genetic information for the cell. Lipids and carbohydrates are structural parts of the cell membrane.

questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
1. Chlordiazepoxide and meprobamate are mild tranquilizers used to relieve tension.
2. Equanil is used to treat infections.
questions.explanation: Some tranquilizers namely, chlordiazepoxide and meprobamate, are relatively mild tranquilizers suitable for relieving tension. Equanil is used in controlling depression and hypertension. Derivatives of barbituric acid viz., veronal, amytal, nembutal, luminal and seconal constitute an important class of tranquilizers. These derivatives are called barbiturates. Barbiturates are hypnotic, i.e., sleep producing agents. Some other substances used as tranquilizers are valium and serotonin. Therefore, only statement 1 is correct.

questions.text: Which of the following is a bacteriostatic antibiotic?
questions.explanation: Antibiotics have either cidal (killing) effect or a static (inhibitor) effect on microbes. Bactericidal antibiotics kill bacteria (e.g., Penicillin, Aminoglycosides, Ofloxacin). Bacteriostatic antibiotics inhibit the growth or reproduction of bacteria (e.g., Tetracycline).

questions.text: Which substance is added to shaving soaps to prevent rapid drying?
questions.explanation: Shaving soaps contain glycerol which prevents rapid drying and maintains moisture. Glycerol is a humectant that attracts and retains moisture, keeping the soap soft and preventing it from drying out quickly. Borax is used as a water softener and preservative. Sodium carbonate is used as a builder. Rosin is used to improve lathering properties.

questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
1. Novolac is a cross-linked polymer.
2. Novolac on heating with formaldehyde forms bakelite.
questions.explanation: Phenol-formaldehyde polymers are the oldest synthetic polymers. These are obtained by the condensation reaction of phenol with formaldehyde in the presence of either an acid or a base catalyst. The reaction starts with the initial formation of o- and/or p-hydroxymethylphenol derivatives, which further react with phenol to form compounds having rings joined to each other through -CH₂ groups. The initial product could be a linear product - Novolac used in paints. Novolac on heating with formaldehyde undergoes cross linking to form an infusible solid mass called bakelite. It is used for making combs, phonograph records, electrical switches and handles of various utensils. Therefore, only statement 2 is correct.

questions.text: Acid used for hydrolysis of sucrose to glucose can be:
questions.explanation: Glucose occurs freely in nature as well as in the combined form. It is present in sweet fruits and honey. Ripe grapes also contain glucose in large amounts. It is prepared as follows: 1. From sucrose (Cane sugar): If sucrose is boiled with dilute HCl or H₂SO₄ in alcoholic solution, glucose and fructose are obtained in equal amounts. 2. From starch: Commercially glucose is obtained by hydrolysis of starch by boiling it with dilute H₂SO₄ at 393 K under pressure.

questions.text: Nucleic acids are polymers of:
questions.explanation: Nucleic acids are the polymers of nucleotides which in turn consist of a base, a pentose sugar and phosphate moiety. Nucleic acids are responsible for the transfer of characters from parents to offsprings. There are two types of nucleic acids - DNA and RNA. DNA contains a five carbon sugar molecule called 2-deoxyribose whereas RNA contains ribose. Both DNA and RNA contain adenine, guanine and cytosine. The fourth base is thymine in DNA and uracil in RNA.

questions.text: The boiling points of aldehydes and ketones are generally:

options.A: Higher than alcohols of similar molecular mass
options.B: Lower than hydrocarbons of similar molecular mass
options.C: Higher than hydrocarbons and ethers but lower than alcohols of similar molecular mass

questions.explanation: Methanal is a gas at room temperature. Ethanal is a volatile liquid. Other aldehydes and ketones are liquid or solid at room temperature. The boiling points of aldehydes and ketones are higher than hydrocarbons and ethers of comparable molecular masses. It is due to weak molecular association in aldehydes and ketones arising out of the dipole-dipole interactions. Also, their boiling points are lower than those of alcohols of similar molecular masses due to absence of intermolecular hydrogen bonding.

questions.text: In alcohols, the boiling point:

options.A: Increases with branching of the carbon chain
options.B: Decreases with branching of the carbon chain
options.C: Is independent of carbon chain length
options.D: Decreases with increase in number of carbon atoms

questions.explanation: Alcohols and phenols consist of two parts, an alkyl/aryl group and a hydroxyl group. The properties of alcohols and phenols are chiefly due to the hydroxyl group. The nature of alkyl and aryl groups simply modify these properties. The boiling points of alcohols and phenols increase with increase in the number of carbon atoms (increase in van der Waals forces). In alcohols, the boiling points decrease with increase of branching in carbon chain (because of decrease in van der Waals forces with decrease in surface area).

questions.text: Henry's law states that at a constant temperature, the solubility of a gas in a liquid is:

options.B: Inversely proportional to the partial pressure of the gas
options.C: Directly proportional to the partial pressure of the gas
options.D: Dependent only on the nature of the solvent

questions.explanation: Henry was the first to give a quantitative relation between pressure and solubility of a gas in a solvent which is known as Henry's law. The law states that at a constant temperature, the solubility of a gas in a liquid is directly proportional to the partial pressure of the gas present above the surface of liquid or solution. If we use the mole fraction of a gas in the solution as a measure of its solubility, then it can be said that the mole fraction of gas in the solution is proportional to the partial pressure of the gas over the solution. The most commonly used form of Henry's law states that 'the partial pressure of the gas in vapour phase (p) is proportional to the mole fraction of the gas (x) in the solution' and is expressed as: p = KHx. Here KH is the Henry's law constant.

questions.text: Goldsmiths blow the outermost zone of a flame with a metallic blowpipe for melting gold and silver. For which one of the following reasons do they use the outermost zone of the flame?

options.A: It is the hottest part providing the most heat.
options.B: This luminous zone doesn't produce any ash.
options.C: It is the least hottest part protecting the design of jewellery.
options.D: Middle zone vaporises gold, leading to loss of metal.

questions.explanation: The substances that vaporise during burning give flames. For example, kerosene oil and molten wax rise through the wick and are vaporised during burning and form flames. Charcoal, on the other hand, does not vaporise and so does not produce a flame. The outermost zone is the hottest part of the flame. This provides for maximum heat while burning, which ensures a quick process. Also, complete combustion takes place in this zone, preventing any sort of blackening of metal. Hence, goldsmiths blow the outermost zone of a flame with a metallic blow-pipe for melting gold and silver. Option B is incorrect. The outermost part is the non-luminous zone of the flame. It is blue in colour. The luminous zone is the middle portion. The luminous zone contains unburnt carbon particles, which can get deposited on the metal if it is used for melting gold and silver. Option C is incorrect. The innermost zone is the least hot and also contains unburnt wax vapours. The outermost zone is the hottest part of the flame. It provides ample heat energy required for the melting of gold. Option D is incorrect. A large amount of heat is required for the vaporisation of gold, which is not generally possible. Also, the middle portion is moderately hot and contains unburnt products due to partial combustion.

questions.text:
Consider the following scenario:
Two inflated balloons are rubbed with the same woollen cloth and brought close to each other. It is observed that the balloons repel each other. In the above scenario, which force is exerted by one of the charged balloons on the other charged balloon?
questions.explanation: When two inflated balloons are rubbed with the same woollen cloth, since the balloons were charged in the same way, we can say that they have acquired similar charges. The two similarly charged balloons repel each other, whereas a charged balloon and the woollen cloth (with which the balloon was rubbed) attract each other. The force exerted by a charged body on another charged body or an uncharged body is called the electrostatic force. It is a non-contact force.

questions.text: Lactometers, which are used for determining the purity of milk, work based on which of the following principles/laws?
questions.explanation: Lactometers, used for determining the purity of milk, are based on Archimedes' principle. Archimedes' principle states that when a body is immersed fully or partially in a fluid, it experiences an upward force that is equal to the weight of the fluid displaced by it. From Archimedes' principle, it is clear that the magnitude of the buoyant force acting on a body at a given place depends on the density of the fluid and the volume of the body immersed in the fluid. The lactometer floats at different depths depending on the density of the milk, which is a function of fat content in the milk.

questions.text: Water can dissolve more substances than any other liquid because:

options.B: It is a good conductor of heat
options.C: It has a high value of specific heat
options.D: It is an oxide of hydrogen

questions.explanation: Water is called the 'universal solvent' because it is capable of dissolving more substances than any other liquid. This is important to every living thing on Earth. It means that wherever water goes, either through the air, the ground, or through our bodies, it takes along valuable chemicals, minerals, and nutrients. It is water's chemical composition and physical attributes that make it such an excellent solvent. Water molecules have a polar arrangement of oxygen and hydrogen atoms—one side (hydrogen) has a positive electrical charge, and the other side (oxygen) has a negative charge. This allows the water molecule to become attracted to many other types of molecules. Water can become so heavily attracted to a different compound, like salt (NaCl), that it can disrupt the attractive forces that hold the sodium and chloride in the salt compound together and, thus, dissolve it.

questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
Statement I: A bar magnet, when suspended freely, has its north and south poles of the magnet point approximately towards the geographic north and south poles, respectively.
Statement II: The force of attraction and repulsion of the magnet is highest at its poles.
Which one of the following is correct with respect to the above statements?

options.A: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct, and Statement II explains Statement I
options.B: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct, and Statement II does not explain Statement I
options.C: Statement I is correct, but Statement II is not correct
options.D: Statement I is not correct, but Statement II is correct

questions.explanation: Statement I is correct: A freely suspended magnet comes to rest along the north-south direction. The end of the magnet that points towards the geographic north direction is called the North-seeking pole or the North pole of the magnet. The other end that points towards the South direction is called the South-seeking pole or the South pole of the magnet. Statement II is correct and does not explain Statement I: The force of attraction and repulsion is maximum at the poles of the magnet because the density of magnetic lines per unit area is maximum at the poles. However, this is not the reason for a freely suspended magnet to always rest in a north-south direction. What is the correct reason for Statement I? A small bar magnet, when suspended freely on its centre of mass, aligns in approximately geographical north-south direction in response to Earth's magnetic field. The Earth acts like a giant bar magnet, with its magnetic south pole near the geographic North Pole and its magnetic north pole near the geographic South Pole. The magnet's north pole is attracted to the Earth's magnetic south pole (near the geographic North Pole), forcing it to align along this axis. Thus, a freely suspended magnet rests along the north-south direction because the Earth itself behaves like a giant magnet.

questions.text:
Consider the following statements:
Statement I: The walls of a large dam are designed with a much wider base compared to their top.
Statement II: The horizontal pressure exerted by water on a dam wall increases with an increase in the depth of water.
Which of the above is correct with respect to the above statements?

options.A: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct, and Statement II explains Statement I.
options.B: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct, but Statement II does not explain Statement I.
options.C: Statement I is correct, but Statement II is not correct.
options.D: Statement I is not correct, but Statement II is correct.

questions.explanation: Statement I is correct: Walls of large dams are constructed with a much wider and stronger base because the lower part of the dam has to withstand very high horizontal forces exerted by water. As depth increases, the pressure and resulting thrust of water on the dam wall also increase. To ensure structural stability and prevent failure, the base is therefore made thicker and broader than the top. Statement II is correct: Water stored in a dam exerts pressure horizontally on the side walls of the dam and vertically on the floor of the reservoir. The horizontal pressure P at the bottom of a column of liquid of height h is given by P = ρgh. That is, hydrostatic pressure due to a fluid (water) increases linearly with depth. It is for this reason that the thickness of the wall of a dam has to be increased with an increase in the depth of the dam. Hence, Statement II correctly explains why dams have wider bases (Statement I).

questions.text:
Consider the following statements with reference to yeasts:
I. They belong to a group of microorganisms called fungi.
II. Carbon dioxide is evolved from food materials spoiled by yeasts.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanation: Yeasts are large, single-celled organisms that rarely form filaments. They are ovoid or spheroid and reproduce by budding or fission. Statement I is correct: Yeast is a type of microorganism that belongs to a group of microorganisms called fungi. Yeast is a unicellular fungus. They are widely distributed in nature. They are somewhat larger than bacteria. The cell length is about 10 μm, and the diameter is about a third of this. Most yeast is spherical or ellipsoidal. Yeasts that multiply by means of 'budding' are known as 'true yeasts'. Statement II is correct: Like other organisms, yeast also respires and breaks down food to release energy for its growth and carries out life processes. During this process, carbon dioxide is released, which forms bubbles that make the dough soft and fluffy. Yeast also produces a small amount of alcohol during this process, which gives the dough a slightly different smell. This special property of yeast is used in the process of making breads, cakes, etc.

questions.text:
With reference to diseases and their causative agents, consider the following pairs:
Disease
I. Measles
II. Cholera
III. Typhoid
IV. Malaria
How many of the pairs given above are correctly matched?
questions.explanation: Pair I is correct: Measles is caused by the Measles virus, which belongs to the Paramyxoviridae family. It is a highly contagious viral disease that spreads through respiratory droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. It affects the skin and respiratory tract. Being viral in nature, it does not respond to antibiotics and is mainly prevented through vaccination (MMR vaccine). Pair II is incorrect: Cholera is not caused by worms. It is an acute diarrhoeal disease caused by the bacterium Vibrio cholerae. The infection spreads mainly through contaminated water or food and leads to severe watery diarrhoea and dehydration. Worms cause diseases such as ascariasis, filariasis (elephantiasis), and hookworm infection, not cholera. Pair III is correct: Typhoid fever is caused by the bacterium Salmonella typhi. It spreads through contaminated food and water, especially in areas with poor sanitation and affects the intestinal tract and bloodstream. Since it is a bacterial disease, it can be treated with appropriate antibiotics. Pair IV is correct: Malaria is caused by protozoan parasites of the genus Plasmodium (such as P. vivax, P. falciparum). These protozoa are transmitted to humans through the bite of an infected female Anopheles mosquito. The protozoa multiply inside liver cells and red blood cells, leading to periodic fever and chills. Being a protozoan disease, malaria requires specific anti-malarial drugs, not antibiotics.

questions.text: When iodine solution is added to a food substance and it turns blue-black in colour, this reaction primarily indicates the presence of which of the following in a food substance?
questions.explanation: When iodine solution is added to a food substance containing starch, it produces a blue-black colour. This happens because iodine molecules get trapped inside the helical (spiral) structure of amylose, which is a major component of starch. This interaction forms a coloured complex, resulting in the characteristic blue-black appearance. This reaction is known as the iodine test for starch and is commonly used to detect the presence of starch in food materials.

questions.text:
Consider the following cell organelles:
I. Cell wall
II. Plastids
III. Golgi apparatus
IV. Cytoplasm
How many of the above are generally present in plant cells but absent in animal cells?
questions.explanation: Plant cells and animal cells are the two major types of eukaryotic cells that form the structural and functional units of plants and animals, respectively. Both possess a well-defined nucleus and membrane-bound organelles such as mitochondria, endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi apparatus, ribosomes and cytoplasm. However, they differ in structure and function due to the distinct lifestyles and nutritional requirements of plants and animals. Option I is correct: The cell wall is a rigid, non-living outer layer located outside the plasma membrane in plant cells. It is primarily composed of cellulose and provides structural support, rigidity, shape, and protection to plant cells. Animal cells do not have a cell wall; instead, they are bounded only by a flexible cell membrane or plasma membrane. Hence, the cell wall is present in plant cells, but absent in animal cells.
