Option II is correct: Plastids are double-membrane-bound organelles found exclusively in plant cells and some algae. They are responsible for key metabolic and biosynthetic functions and include chloroplasts (for photosynthesis), chromoplasts (for pigment storage), and leucoplasts (for storage of starch, proteins, and fats). Animal cells lack plastids because they do not perform photosynthesis. Therefore, plastids are present only in plant cells and are absent in animal cells. Option III is incorrect: The Golgi apparatus is present in both plant and animal cells. It is involved in the modification, packaging, and transport of proteins and lipids. In plant cells, the Golgi bodies are often referred to as dictyosomes, but they are structurally and functionally the same organelle. Since it occurs in both cell types, it is not exclusive to plant cells. Option IV is incorrect: The cytoplasm is the main arena of cellular activities in both plant and animal cells. Various chemical reactions occur in it to keep the cell in the 'living state'. Cytoplasm is the semi-fluid, jelly-like substance present between the plasma membrane and the nucleus. It contains cell organelles and is the site of most metabolic and biochemical reactions. Since cytoplasm is essential for basic cellular functioning, it is present in all living cells, including both plants and animals.

questions.text:
With reference to differences between xylem and phloem tissues in plants, which of the following statements is/are correct?
I. Xylem primarily transports water and minerals from roots to other parts of the plant, whereas phloem primarily transports prepared food from leaves to different parts of the plant.
II. Transport through xylem is mainly bidirectional, whereas transport through phloem is always unidirectional.
III. Most of the structural elements of xylem are dead at maturity, while most structural elements of phloem are living tissues.
questions.explanation: Vascular tissues are specialised conducting tissues in plants that help in the transport of water, minerals, and food, and also provide mechanical support. They form the plant's internal transport system and are mainly of two types: xylem and phloem. Together, xylem and phloem form vascular bundles, which are present in roots and stems. These tissues play a crucial role in the growth, survival, and proper functioning of higher plants. Statement I is correct: Xylem and phloem are the two main vascular tissues in plants with distinct transport functions. Xylem is responsible for transporting water and dissolved mineral salts from roots to the stem, leaves, and other aerial parts of the plant. Phloem transports prepared food (photosynthates) synthesised in the leaves to different parts of the plant. Statement II is incorrect: Transport through xylem is essentially unidirectional, occurring mainly from roots to aerial parts due to root pressure, capillary action, and transpiration pull. In contrast, phloem transport is bidirectional, as food can move both upward and downward depending on source-sink relationships. Statement III is correct: Xylem is a conducting tissue that conducts water and salts upward from roots to leaves. Xylem is composed of tracheids, vessels, fibres and xylem parenchyma. Most structural elements of xylem, such as tracheids, vessels, and xylem fibres (except xylem parenchyma), are dead at maturity, providing efficient conduction and mechanical support. Phloem is composed of sieve tube elements, companion cells, phloem fibres, and phloem parenchyma. In contrast to xylem, most phloem elements, such as sieve tube elements, companion cells, and phloem parenchyma (except phloem fibres) are living tissues, which are essential for active food transport.

questions.text:
With reference to the ongoing war in West Asia, which of the following statements with respect to the differences between ballistic missiles and cruise missiles is/are incorrect?
I. Compared to Ballistic missiles, Cruise missiles are generally harder to detect by radar.
II. Cruise missiles can only be used to deliver conventional warheads, whereas ballistic missiles can carry both nuclear and conventional warheads.
questions.explanation: Missiles are a core component of modern military capability and are classified based on their flight path, propulsion system, altitude, and guidance mechanism. Among them, ballistic missiles and cruise missiles represent two fundamentally different technologies, each designed for distinct strategic and tactical roles. Statement I is correct: Cruise missiles are difficult to detect because they fly low and can use terrain for concealment. Ballistic missiles, due to their high-altitude trajectory and speed, are generally easier to detect and track by radar and satellite systems once launched. Ballistic missiles are initially powered by one or more rocket stages, after which they follow a high-arching, unpowered (ballistic) trajectory through space before descending toward their target. In contrast, cruise missiles are jet-powered and maintain a steady, low-altitude flight path throughout their journey to the target. Cruise missiles remain within the atmosphere for the duration of their flight and can fly as low as a few meters off the ground. Flying low to the surface of the earth expends more fuel but makes a cruise missile very difficult to detect. Statement II is incorrect: Both cruise and ballistic missiles are capable of delivering either conventional or nuclear warheads. For instance, the BrahMos missile is a supersonic cruise missile capable of carrying both conventional and nuclear warheads. The Agni series (like Agni-V, Agni-IV) is a nuclear-capable ballistic missile, designed specifically for strategic deterrence.

questions.text: What is the primary function of a moderator in a nuclear reactor?

options.A: To absorb excess neutrons and stop the chain reaction.
options.B: To convert nuclear energy directly into electrical energy.
options.C: To slow down fast-moving neutrons produced during nuclear fission.
options.D: To remove heat from the reactor core to prevent it from excessive heating.

questions.explanation: In a nuclear reactor, energy is released when a heavy nucleus like uranium splits. This process is called nuclear fission. When fission occurs, it releases a large amount of energy and also fast-moving neutrons. For a sustained and controlled chain reaction, neutrons must move slowly. Such neutrons are called thermal neutrons. Moderator is a substance used to slow down the neutrons produced by fission to sustain the chain reaction. Common moderator materials include graphite, heavy water, and ordinary water.

questions.text:
Recently, the Kalpakkam fast breeder reactor attained criticality. With reference to the above news, consider the following statements:
Statement I: Naturally occurring thorium can be directly used as fuel in nuclear reactors for the generation of electrical power.
Statement II: Naturally occurring thorium is a 'fertile' material that can be converted into a 'fissile material.'
Which one of the following is correct with respect to the above statements?

options.A: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct, and Statement II explains Statement I
options.B: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct, but Statement II does not explain Statement I
options.C: Statement I is correct, but Statement II is not correct
options.D: Statement I is not correct, but Statement II is correct

questions.explanation: Thorium is a silvery, slightly radioactive metal commonly found in igneous rocks and heavy mineral sands. Thorium-232 is the only naturally occurring isotope of thorium. India has the largest deposits of Thorium in the world, roughly a quarter of the world's thorium. Together, Kerala and Odisha account for over 70 per cent of India's thorium. The third stage of India's nuclear power programme envisages the use of thorium for power generation. Statement I is incorrect: Thorium by itself cannot be directly used as a fuel in nuclear reactors for the generation of power. Thorium has to be converted to Uranium-233 in a reactor before it can be used as fuel. Statement II is correct: Thorium is not a fissile material. Rather, it is fissionable fertile material that has to be converted to fissile material U-233. When irradiated, thorium-232 undergoes a series of nuclear reactions, eventually forming uranium-233, which can then be split to release energy to power a nuclear reactor. Thus, Thorium will produce fissile U-233, which will be used as fuel to generate nuclear energy. Thus, thorium in itself is not directly a nuclear fuel, but it can be used to create one.

questions.text:
Consider the following diseases:
I. Sickle-cell anaemia
II. Thalassemia
III. Tuberculosis
IV. Phenylketonuria
How many of the above are generally referred to as genetic disorders?
questions.explanation: Broadly, genetic disorders are classified into Mendelian disorders and chromosomal disorders. Mendelian disorders arise due to a mutation or alteration in a single gene and are inherited according to the principles of inheritance, with their transmission pattern traceable through pedigree analysis. Common examples of Mendelian disorders include haemophilia, cystic fibrosis, sickle-cell anaemia, colour blindness, phenylketonuria, and thalassemia. Option I is correct: Sickle-cell anaemia is an autosomal recessive genetic disorder transmitted to offspring when both parents are carriers (heterozygous) of the defective gene. The condition is governed by a single gene with two alleles, HbA (normal) and HbS (mutant). Individuals with the heterozygous genotype (HbA HbS) usually appear normal but act as carriers of the disease and may pass the mutant gene to their children with a 50% probability, a condition known as the sickle-cell trait. Option II is correct: Thalassemia is also an autosomal recessive blood disease transmitted from parents to the offspring when both the partners are carriers for the gene (or heterozygous). The defect could be due to either a mutation or a deletion, which ultimately results in a reduced rate of synthesis of one of the globin chains (α and β chains) that make up haemoglobin. Option III is incorrect: Tuberculosis (TB) is not a genetic disorder because it is not caused by a mutation in genes or chromosomes. It is an infectious disease caused by the bacterium Mycobacterium tuberculosis. The disease spreads through airborne droplets when an infected person coughs or sneezes. Option IV is correct: Phenylketonuria is an inborn error of metabolism and is also inherited as an autosomal recessive trait. The affected individual lacks an enzyme that converts the amino acid phenylalanine into tyrosine. As a result of this, phenylalanine is accumulated and converted into phenylpyruvic acid and other derivatives. Accumulation of these in the brain results in mental retardation. These are also excreted through urine because of their poor absorption by the kidneys.

questions.text: In amoeba, food is digested in which part?
questions.explanation: In an amoeba, food is digested within a membrane-bound structure called the food vacuole. The process follows these steps: Ingestion: The amoeba extends its pseudopodia to surround and engulf food particles. Digestion: Once the food is trapped, it forms a food vacuole. Digestive enzymes (often from lysosomes) are secreted into this vacuole to break down complex substances into simpler, soluble molecules. Absorption & Assimilation: The digested nutrients then diffuse out of the vacuole into the cytoplasm for use by the cell.

questions.text: In which mode of nutrition an organism derives food from the body of another living organism without killing it?
questions.explanation: Parasitic nutrition is a type of heterotrophic nutrition where an organism, known as a parasite, lives on or inside the body of another living organism, called the host, to obtain its nutrients. Crucially, the parasite derives its food without immediately killing the host, although it often causes harm or weakens it over time. Examples include: Plants: Cuscuta (dodder) and mistletoe. Animals: Tapeworms, lice, leeches, ticks, and Plasmodium (the malaria parasite).

questions.text: Which part of nephron allows the selective re-absorption of useful substances into the blood capillaries?
questions.explanation: Selective re-absorption is the process by which essential substances—such as glucose, amino acids, salts, and water—are reclaimed from the filtrate and returned to the blood capillaries. This process occurs primarily in the tubule of the nephron, which includes the proximal convoluted tubule (PCT), the loop of Henle, and the distal convoluted tubule (DCT). The Proximal Convoluted Tubule (PCT) is responsible for the majority of this re-absorption, reclaiming nearly 100% of glucose and amino acids and about 65-70% of water and electrolytes. The tubule's lining consists of specialized epithelial cells (often with microvilli to increase surface area) that use active and passive transport to move these 'useful substances' back into the surrounding peritubular capillaries.

questions.text:
Consider the following statements.
I. Pyruvate may be converted into ethanol and carbon dioxide in presence of oxygen.
II. Pyruvate may be converted into carbon dioxide and water in absence of oxygen.
III. Pyruvate may be converted into lactic acid in lack of oxygen.
Which of the above statement(s) is/are correct?
questions.explanation: The food material taken in during the process of nutrition is used in cells to provide energy for various life processes. Diverse organisms do this in different ways - some use oxygen to break-down glucose completely into carbon dioxide and water, some use other pathways that do not involve oxygen. In all cases, the first step is the break-down of glucose, a six-carbon molecule, into a three-carbon molecule called pyruvate. This process takes place in the cytoplasm. Further, the pyruvate may be converted into ethanol and carbon dioxide. This process takes place in yeast during fermentation. Since this process takes place in the absence of air (oxygen), it is called anaerobic respiration. Breakdown of pyruvate using oxygen takes place in the mitochondria. This process breaks up the three-carbon pyruvate molecule to give three molecules of carbon dioxide. The other product is water. Since this process takes place in the presence of air (oxygen), it is called aerobic respiration. The release of energy in this aerobic process is a lot greater than in the anaerobic process. Sometimes, when there is a lack of oxygen in our muscle cells, another pathway for the break-down of pyruvate is taken. Here the pyruvate is converted into lactic acid which is also a three-carbon molecule. This build-up of lactic acid in our muscles during sudden activity causes cramps. Therefore, only statement III is correct.

questions.text:
Consider the following.
I. Dendrite - Information is acquired
II. Cell body - Impulse converted into a chemical signal
III. Axon end - Information travels as an electrical impulse
Which of the above is/are correctly matched?
questions.explanation: Based on the analysis of neural structure and function: Dendrite - Information is acquired: Correct. Dendrites (specifically dendritic tips) receive incoming information or chemical signals from other neurons or receptors. Cell body - Impulse converted into a chemical signal: Incorrect. While the cell body processes information, the conversion of an electrical impulse into a chemical signal occurs at the synapse/axon end, not the cell body. Axon end - Information travels as an electrical impulse: Incorrect. The electrical impulse travels down the axon, but at the axon end (or synapse), it is converted into a chemical signal (neurotransmitters) to cross to the next neuron.

questions.text:
Consider the following statements.
I. The DNA in the cell nucleus is the information source for making proteins.
II. Different proteins eventually lead to altered body designs.
Identify the correct statement/s.
questions.explanation: Statement I is correct - The DNA within the nucleus acts as the master blueprint or information source, directing the cell on how to construct specific proteins. Statement II is correct - Changes in the DNA, or the production of different proteins based on that DNA, lead to variations in the functional proteins being produced, which over time can cause changes in the organism's traits and overall body design.

questions.text: The consistency of DNA copying during reproduction is important

options.C: for the maintenance of body design features.

questions.explanation: DNA copying (replication) during reproduction is essential because it ensures that the genetic information from parents is accurately passed on to their offspring. This consistency allows the offspring to inherit the same fundamental body design, traits, and cellular structures that are necessary for survival within their specific environment or ecological niche. While variations (errors) can occur, the overall consistency is vital for maintaining the stability of the species' characteristics.

questions.text: Which of the following is true for Vegetatively propagated plants?
questions.explanation: Vegetative propagation is a form of asexual reproduction where new plants are grown from the vegetative parts of a single parent plant, such as the stems, roots, or leaves. Because this process does not involve the fusion of gametes (fertilization) or genetic recombination, the resulting offspring are clones of the parent. Consequently, they possess the exact same genetic makeup and are genetically similar (or identical) to the parent plant.

questions.text: The main function of abscisic acid in plants is

options.D: To increase the length of cells.

questions.explanation: Abscisic acid (ABA) is primarily known as a growth-inhibiting hormone or 'stress hormone' in plants. It serves as an antagonist to growth-promoting hormones like auxins, gibberellins, and cytokinins. Its main physiological functions include: Inhibiting Growth: It slows down plant metabolism and growth processes, particularly during unfavorable environmental conditions. Inducing Dormancy: It promotes dormancy in seeds and buds, ensuring they do not germinate or sprout until conditions are favorable. Stomatal Closure: During water stress (drought), it triggers the closing of stomata to reduce water loss through transpiration. Abscission and Senescence: It promotes the falling of leaves and fruits (abscission) and the aging of plant tissues (senescence).

questions.text: Salivation is controlled by which part of the brain?
questions.explanation: Salivation is an involuntary action (autonomic reflex) primarily regulated by the medulla oblongata. The medulla contains specialized clusters of neurons called salivary nuclei (specifically the inferior salivary nucleus) that send signals via cranial nerves to the salivary glands to trigger the production of spit in response to stimuli like the smell or taste of food. It also manages other vital involuntary functions such as breathing, heart rate, blood pressure, and vomiting.

questions.text:
Consider the following statements about the basic parts of a cell:
1. The cell membrane controls the entry and exit of substances into the cell.
2. The nucleus regulates cell activities and carries hereditary information.
3. The cytoplasm is the site of most biochemical reactions.
4. The cell wall is present in both plant and animal cells, providing rigidity.
Which of the above are correct?
questions.explanation: Statement 1: Correct. The cell membrane is selectively permeable, controlling the entry and exit of substances, maintaining homeostasis. Statement 2: Correct. The nucleus contains DNA and directs protein synthesis and regulates cell activities. Statement 3: Correct. Organelles and enzymes in the cytoplasm enable metabolism and most biochemical reactions. Statement 4: Incorrect. The cell wall is present only in plant cells (and some bacteria), not in animal cells, providing rigidity and protection.

questions.text:
Consider the following statements about plant and animal cells:
1. Plastids are tiny rod-shaped structures found in plant cells, some of which (chloroplasts) contain chlorophyll for photosynthesis.
2. In non-green parts of plants, plastids help in storage of substances.
3. Plant cells have a large vacuole that stores substances, removes waste, and maintains cell shape.
4. Animal cells always have large vacuoles similar to plant cells.
Which of the above are correct?
questions.explanation: Statement 1: Correct. Chloroplasts are plastids that enable photosynthesis, giving plants their green color. Plastids containing chlorophyll, known as chloroplasts, are double-membraned organelles found in the mesophyll cells of green plant leaves. They capture sunlight via chlorophyll pigments, converting light energy into chemical energy through photosynthesis. Statement 2: Correct. Non-chlorophyll plastids (like leucoplasts) store starch, oils, and proteins. Plastids lacking chlorophyll, such as leucoplasts (colorless) and chromoplasts (colored), dominate in roots, tubers, fruits, and flowers. Leucoplasts store starch (e.g., potato tubers), proteins (e.g., pulses), or oils (e.g., castor seeds), acting as reservoirs for nutrients. Statement 3: Correct. Vacuoles maintain turgidity, store nutrients, and remove waste, giving strength and support. Plant cells feature a prominent central vacuole occupying 50-90% of cell volume, bounded by a tonoplast membrane. Filled with cell sap, it performs multiple functions: maintains turgor pressure for structural rigidity (preventing wilting), stores water/nutrients/ions, degrades waste via hydrolytic enzymes, and detoxifies harmful substances. Statement 4: Incorrect. Vacuoles in animal cells are usually absent or very small, unlike the large central vacuole in plants. Animal cells lack a large central vacuole; instead, they have small, temporary vacuoles (or vesicles) for specific tasks like digestion (food vacuoles in protozoa) or osmoregulation (contractile vacuoles in Amoeba). These are far smaller (often < 5% volume) and multiple, not centralized.

questions.text: Which of the following features helps neurons (nerve cells) to transmit messages quickly in the body?
questions.explanation: Neurons transmit messages rapidly due to their unique elongated and branched structure, which forms an efficient network for electrical and chemical signaling across the body. Neurons, or nerve cells, are the body's primary signaling units, specialized for rapid communication. A typical neuron consists of a cell body (soma) housing the nucleus, dendrites (short, branched extensions receiving signals), and a long axon (tail-like fiber transmitting signals away). This design contrasts with typical round cells, enabling extensive connectivity—some axons stretch up to 1 meter in humans, like those in the sciatic nerve. The elongated axon acts as a high-speed conduit for action potentials (electrical impulses traveling at 1-100 m/s), insulated by myelin for faster conduction via saltatory movement. Branched dendrites and axon terminals allow one neuron to synapse with thousands of others, forming neural circuits for quick relay.

questions.text: Which of the following is the correct sequence of levels of organisation in a living organism?

options.A: Organ → Tissue → Cell → Organ system → Organism
options.B: Cell → Tissue → Organ → Organ system → Organism
options.C: Tissue → Cell → Organ → Organism → Organ system
options.D: Cell → Organ → Tissue → Organ system → Organism

questions.explanation: The accurate hierarchy is Cell → Tissue → Organ → Organ System → Organism (Option B). Living organisms exhibit a hierarchical level of organization, building from the simplest structural and functional unit—the cell—up to the complete organism, enabling complex life processes. The levels of organisations are: Cell → Tissue → Organ → Organ system → Organism. These levels of organisation help us understand how simple building blocks like cells come together to form a complex living being.

questions.text:
Match the Following
Column A (Microorganism)
1. Amoeba (Protozoa)
2. Mould (Fungi)
3. Algae
4. Bacteria
Column B (Characteristic)
A. Branched filament without chlorophyll, brush-like structure
B. Single cell, irregular shape, moves
C. Spherical, presence of chlorophyll (green pigment)
D. Spherical/comma/spiral/rod shaped, with hair-like projections
questions.explanation: Amoeba (Protozoa): Single-celled eukaryotic with changing irregular shape via pseudopodia (false feet) for amoeboid movement and engulfing food (phagocytosis). Common in freshwater; causes dysentery (amoebic dysentery) via Entamoeba histolytica. Mould (Fungi): Multicellular fungi (e.g., Rhizopus, Aspergillus) forming hyphae (branched filaments) in mycelium; non-chlorophyllous, heterotrophic (saprophytic/parasitic). Brush-like sporangiophores release spores; source of antibiotics like penicillin. Algae: Autotrophic (photosynthetic) due to chlorophyll; unicellular forms like Chlamydomonas (spherical/oval). Range from microscopic to seaweeds; produce oxygen, base of aquatic food chains. Bacteria: Prokaryotes lacking nucleus; shapes: coccus (spherical), bacillus (rod), vibrio (comma), spirillum (spiral). Flagella (hair-like) enable motility; peptidoglycan cell wall. Causes diseases like cholera (Vibrio), tetanus (Clostridium).

questions.text: Why are leguminous crops like peas and beans often grown in crop rotation?

options.A: They increase water content in the soil
options.B: They help in nitrogen fixation through bacteria in root nodules
options.D: They reduce sunlight requirement for crops

questions.explanation: Leguminous crops like peas and beans are grown in crop rotation primarily to naturally enrich soil fertility through symbiotic nitrogen fixation, reducing the need for chemical fertilizers. Legumes form root nodules housing Rhizobium bacteria (symbiotic, Gram-negative), which convert atmospheric N₂ into ammonia via nitrogenase enzyme—a process requiring anaerobic conditions inside nodules' leghemoglobin (pink pigment binds O₂). This biologically fixed nitrogen (up to 200 kg/ha) gets incorporated into soil as nitrates, benefiting subsequent non-legume crops like wheat or maize in rotation.

questions.text: Spirulina is called a superfood mainly because it is rich in:
questions.explanation: Spirulina earns its 'superfood' status primarily due to its exceptionally high protein content, making it one of the most nutrient-dense plant-based sources available. Spirulina, a blue-green cyanobacterium (microalgae), boasts 60-70% protein by dry weight—higher than soybeans (35-40%) or meat (20-30%)—with all essential amino acids, ideal for vegetarians and athletes. It also provides vitamin B12 (rare in plants), beta-carotene, iron, and antioxidants like phycocyanin, while being low in fat (< 8%) and carbs (~20%). This complete profile supports immunity, energy, and detoxification. NASA uses it for astronauts, and it is also promoted by WHO to be very effective against malnutrition.

questions.text:
Consider the following statements about diseases in India:
1. Non-communicable diseases (NCDs) such as cancer, diabetes, and asthma are not caused by pathogens and do not spread from person to person.
2. Communicable diseases are caused by pathogens and can spread from one person to another.
3. The rise of NCDs in India is linked to lifestyle changes such as processed food consumption, reduced physical activity, and longer lifespans.
4. Today, most deaths in India are caused by communicable diseases.
Which of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanation: Statement 1: Correct. Non-communicable diseases are linked to lifestyle, environment, and genetics. Examples: cancer, diabetes, asthma. Statement 2: Correct. Pathogens include bacteria, viruses, fungi, parasites. Examples: typhoid, dengue, flu, chickenpox, COVID-19. Statement 3: Correct. Urbanization, processed food, sedentary habits, and longevity have increased NCD prevalence in India. Statement 4: Incorrect. Today, NCDs account for the majority of deaths in India, overtaking communicable diseases.

questions.text:
Match the Following:
List – I (Disease)
A. Measles
B. Tuberculosis (TB)
C. Ascariasis
D. Malaria
Column B (Causative Agent)
1. Protozoa
2. Bacteria
3. Worm
4. Virus
questions.explanation: Measles is caused by a Virus (4). Tuberculosis (TB) is caused by Bacteria (2). Ascariasis is caused by a parasitic Worm (3). Malaria is caused by a Protozoan parasite (1).

questions.text:
Consider the following statements about antibiotics and antibiotics-resistance:
1. Antibiotics are effective against bacterial infections and have saved millions of lives since their discovery.
2. Indiscriminate use of antibiotics has led to the phenomenon of antibiotic resistance.
3. Antibiotic resistance makes common infections easier to treat and reduces complications
4. Antibiotic resistance occurs when bacteria survive and multiply despite treatment with a given antibiotic.
Which of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanation: Statement 1: Correct. Since penicillin's discovery (1928), antibiotics have revolutionized medicine, saving millions of lives. Statement 2: Correct. Overuse, misuse (self-medication, incomplete courses), and use in livestock have accelerated resistance. Statement 3: Incorrect. Resistance makes infections harder to treat, increases risk of complications, prolonged illness, and even death. Statement 4: Correct. Resistant strains adapt, multiply, and spread, making infections harder to cure.

questions.text:
Which of the following statements regarding pollination in flowering plants is/are correct?
1. Pollination is the transfer of pollen grains from stamens to carpels.
2. Pollination always results in fertilisation.
3. Agents like wind, insects, birds, and bats can help in pollination.
questions.explanation: Statement 1 (Correct): Pollination is accurately defined as the transfer of pollen grains from the anther (male part of stamen) to the stigma (female part of carpel), initiating the reproductive process in angiosperms. This can be self-pollination (within same flower/plant) or cross-pollination (between plants). Statement 2 (Incorrect): Pollination does not guarantee fertilization; it merely deposits pollen on the stigma. Successful fertilization requires pollen tube growth through style to reach the ovule for double fertilization (syngamy + triple fusion), which fails if pollen is incompatible, non-viable, or blocked. Statement 3 (Correct): Pollination agents (pollinators) include abiotic (wind/anemophily, water/hydrophily) and biotic (insects/entomophily, birds/ornithophily, bats/chiropterophily), each adapted to flower traits like light sticky pollen (wind) or nectar/scent (animals).

questions.text:
Which of the following statements about ecosystems is/are correct?
1. An ecosystem consists of interactions between biotic and abiotic components.
2. Aquatic ecosystems include forests and grasslands.
3. Farmland is an example of a human-made ecosystem.
questions.explanation: Statement 1 (Correct): An ecosystem comprises biotic components (producers like plants, consumers like animals, decomposers like fungi/bacteria) interacting with abiotic factors (sunlight, soil, water, temperature) through energy flow and nutrient cycles, forming a functional unit. Statement 2 (Incorrect): Aquatic ecosystems include freshwater (ponds, rivers, wetlands) and marine (oceans, coral reefs) types, characterized by water as the primary medium. Forests (tropical, temperate) and grasslands are terrestrial ecosystems, relying on soil and air. Statement 3 (Correct): Farmland (agroecosystem) is a human-modified ecosystem where crops/livestock interact with managed abiotic factors (irrigation, fertilizers). Unlike natural ecosystems, it's artificial but follows ecological principles like food chains.

questions.text:
Match the following types of interactions with their correct definitions:
List – I (Type of Interaction)
A. Mutualism
B. Commensalism
C. Parasitism
Column B (Definition)
1. One organism exploits the host for resources, causing harm without immediate death
2. Reciprocal interdependence ensuring survival and fitness of both organisms
3. One organism derives benefit without measurable gain or loss to the other
questions.explanation: Mutualism: Reciprocal interdependence ensuring survival and fitness of both organisms: Obligate (+/+) relationship where both species gain (e.g., bees pollinating flowers—bee gets nectar, flower gets pollen transfer; lichens: fungus protection + algae photosynthesis). Commensalism: One organism derives benefit without measurable gain or loss to the other: (+/0) interaction where host neither gains nor loses (e.g., barnacles on whale—barnacles get mobility/food, whale unaffected; epiphytes on trees). Parasitism: One organism exploits the host for resources, causing harm without immediate death: (+/-) where parasite thrives at host's expense (e.g., tapeworms in human gut—parasite nutrients, host malnutrition; Plasmodium causing malaria).

questions.text: What is meant by geodiversity?

options.A: Variety of living organisms on Earth
options.B: Variety of landforms, rocks, soils and the processes shaping them
options.C: Study of climate and weather patterns

questions.explanation: There are various types of landforms, rocks, soils, etc., on Earth. This variety along with the processes that shape and alter them is called geodiversity. It helps create unique habitats where different types of life can thrive. The non-living parts of nature, like soil, rocks, and water, aren't just a background—they help shape the story of life itself.

questions.text: In animals, the development of the zygote into an embryo can occur either inside or outside the body. Which of the following statements best explains the evolutionary advantage of internal development compared to external development?

options.A: Internal development ensures genetic variation in offspring.
options.B: Internal development provides better protection and nourishment to the developing embryo.
options.C: Internal development allows offspring to be exact copies of the parent.
options.D: Internal development reduces the need for sexual reproduction.

questions.explanation: Internal development (seen in mammals and some reptiles) provides the embryo with a controlled environment, protection from predators, and direct nourishment via structures like the placenta. This increases the chances of survival compared to external development (like in fish or amphibians, where eggs are laid outside the body and exposed to environmental risks). Option A is incorrect because genetic variation arises from sexual reproduction, not from whether development is internal or external. Option C is incorrect because exact copies of the parent are produced in asexual reproduction, not in sexual reproduction with internal development. Option D is incorrect because internal development still requires sexual reproduction; it does not eliminate the need for it.

questions.text:
Match the Following: List-I with List-II.
List – I (Scientist)
A. Robert Hooke
B. Antonie van Leeuwenhoek
C. Robert Brown
D. Purkinje
E. Schleiden & Schwann
List – II (Contribution)
1. Coined the term 'protoplasm' for the living substance of the cell
2. First observed cells in a cork slice using a primitive microscope (1665)
3. Discovered free-living microscopic organisms in pond water (1674)
4. Discovered the nucleus in the cell (1831)
5. Proposed that all plants and animals are composed of cells (Cell Theory)
questions.explanation: Robert Hooke first observed cells in a cork slice using a primitive microscope in 1665 (2). Antonie van Leeuwenhoek discovered free-living microscopic organisms in pond water in 1674 (3). Robert Brown discovered the nucleus in the cell in 1831 (4). Purkinje coined the term 'protoplasm' for the living substance of the cell (1). Schleiden and Schwann proposed that all plants and animals are composed of cells (Cell Theory) (5).

questions.text: Which of the following statements about diffusion and osmosis is correct?

options.A: Diffusion always requires energy input from the cell.
options.B: Oxygen enters the cell by diffusion when its concentration inside the cell decreases.
options.C: Osmosis refers to the movement of gases like CO₂ and O₂ across the cell membrane.
options.D: Diffusion occurs only in water molecules, while osmosis occurs in all substances.

questions.explanation: Option A: Incorrect. Diffusion is a passive process; it does not require energy from the cell. Substances move spontaneously from high concentration to low concentration. Option B: Correct. When oxygen concentration inside the cell is low compared to the outside environment, oxygen diffuses into the cell. This is a vital process for cellular respiration. Option C: Incorrect. Osmosis specifically refers to the movement of water molecules across a selectively permeable membrane, not gases. Gases like CO₂ and O₂ move by diffusion. Option D: Incorrect. Diffusion applies to all types of molecules (gases, solutes, etc.), while osmosis is a special case of diffusion restricted to water molecules.

questions.text:
Which of the following statements regarding osmosis is/are correct?
1. In a hypotonic solution, water enters the cell and it may swell.
2. In a hypertonic solution, water leaves the cell causing it to shrink.
3. In an isotonic solution, water moves across the membrane but there is no net movement.
questions.explanation: Statement 1 - Correct: If the medium surrounding the cell has a higher water concentration than the cell (hypotonic solution), the cell will gain water by osmosis. Water molecules are free to pass across the cell membrane in both directions, but more water will come into the cell than will leave. The net result is that water enters the cell, and the cell is likely to swell up. Statement 2 - Correct: If the medium has a lower concentration of water than the cell (hypertonic solution), the cell will lose water by osmosis. Again, water crosses the cell membrane in both directions, but this time more water leaves the cell than enters it. Therefore the cell will shrink. Statement 3 - Correct: If the medium has exactly the same water concentration as the cell (isotonic solution), there will be no net movement of water across the cell membrane. Water crosses the cell membrane in both directions, but the amount going in is the same as the amount going out, so there is no overall movement of water. The cell will stay the same size.

questions.text: What is the process called by which a cell engulfs food or other materials from its surroundings?
questions.explanation: Endocytosis is the process by which a cell takes in food or other materials by engulfing them using its flexible cell membrane. The cell membrane invaginates (folds inward) around the target substance, forming a vesicle that pinches off and transports the material into the cytoplasm for digestion or use. This energy-requiring process contrasts with passive methods like diffusion (simple molecule spread) or osmosis (water movement across membranes). Example: Amoeba acquires its food through endocytosis.

questions.text: Which of the following statements about chromosomes and DNA is correct?

options.A: Chromosomes are visible at all times in the cell.
options.B: Chromosomes are composed only of DNA molecules.
options.C: Genes are functional segments of DNA that carry hereditary information.
options.D: Chromatin material is visible only during cell division.

questions.explanation: Option A: Incorrect. Chromosomes are visible as rod-shaped structures only when the cell is about to divide. In a non-dividing cell, DNA exists as chromatin material. Option B: Incorrect. Chromosomes are composed of DNA and proteins (histones), not DNA alone. Option C: Correct. Genes are specific functional segments of DNA that encode information for inheritance and control cellular functions. This is the fundamental unit of heredity. Option D: Incorrect. Chromatin material is visible in a non-dividing cell as an entangled mass of thread-like structures. During division, chromatin condenses into distinct chromosomes.

questions.text: Which of the following statements correctly distinguishes prokaryotic cells from eukaryotic cells?

options.A: Prokaryotic cells have a well-defined nuclear membrane, while eukaryotic cells lack one.
options.B: Prokaryotic cells lack a nuclear membrane and most cytoplasmic organelles, while eukaryotic cells possess them.
options.C: Prokaryotic cells contain plastids for photosynthesis, while eukaryotic cells use vesicles.
options.D: Prokaryotic cells cannot perform any cellular functions without organelles.

questions.explanation: Option A: Incorrect. It is the eukaryotic cells that have a well-defined nuclear membrane, while prokaryotic cells lack one. Option B: Correct. Prokaryotes (like bacteria) do not have a nuclear membrane or most organelles. Their functions are carried out by less organized parts of the cytoplasm. Eukaryotes (plants, animals, fungi, protists) have a nucleus and membrane-bound organelles like mitochondria, plastids, and Golgi bodies. Option C: Incorrect. In prokaryotic photosynthetic bacteria, chlorophyll is associated with membranous vesicles, not plastids. Plastids are found only in eukaryotic cells. Option D: Incorrect. Prokaryotic cells can perform all essential life functions (respiration, photosynthesis, reproduction) even without membrane-bound organelles, using simpler structures.

questions.text: Which of the following statements correctly explains why viruses are not considered fully living organisms?

options.A: Viruses can photosynthesize and produce their own energy.
options.B: Viruses have cell membranes and organelles like living cells.
options.C: Viruses lack membranes and cannot carry out life processes independently.
options.D: Viruses can move and respire outside a host.

questions.explanation: Viruses are unique biological entities because they do not possess the fundamental structures of living cells, such as a cell membrane or cytoplasm. This structural deficiency prevents them from performing independent life processes like metabolism, growth, or reproduction. They remain inert outside a host organism. Only after entering a living cell can they hijack the host's cellular machinery to replicate and produce new virus particles. Therefore, viruses occupy a gray area between living and non-living entities—they are inert outside a host and show characteristics of life only within one.

questions.text: Which statement best describes the general role of the endoplasmic reticulum (ER)?

options.A: ER is a network of membranes involved in protein and lipid synthesis, as well as in membrane formation and enzyme/hormone production.
options.B: ER only stores waste and toxins within the cell.
options.D: ER is a rigid structure and does not form a network in cells.

questions.explanation: The ER is a dynamic network system present in all active cells. It synthesizes proteins (RER) and lipids (SER), helps in membrane biogenesis, and produces molecules that act as enzymes and hormones. The endoplasmic reticulum (ER) is a large network of membrane-bound tubes and sheets. It looks like long tubules or round or oblong bags (vesicles). There are two types of ER- rough endoplasmic reticulum (RER) and smooth endoplasmic reticulum (SER). RER looks rough under a microscope because it has particles called ribosomes attached to its surface. The ribosomes, which are present in all active cells, are the sites of protein manufacture. The SER helps in the manufacture of fat molecules, or lipids, important for cell function. Some of these proteins and lipids help in building the cell membrane. This process is known as membrane biogenesis.

questions.text: How does meiosis differ from mitosis?

options.A: Meiosis produces two identical cells, mitosis produces four different cells
options.B: Meiosis occurs in body cells, mitosis occurs in reproductive cells
options.C: Meiosis produces four cells with half the chromosomes, mitosis produces two identical cells
options.D: Meiosis does not involve cell division, mitosis does

questions.explanation: The process of cell division by which most of the cells divide for growth is called mitosis. In this process, each cell called mother cell divides to form two identical daughter cells. The daughter cells have the same number of chromosomes as mother cell. It helps in growth and repair of tissues in organisms. Specific cells of reproductive organs or tissues in animals and plants divide to form gametes, which after fertilisation give rise to offspring. They divide by a different process called meiosis which involves two consecutive divisions. When a cell divides by meiosis it produces four new cells instead of just two. The new cells only have half the number of chromosomes than that of the mother cells. Meiosis involves two consecutive divisions in reproductive cells, producing four daughter cells with half the number of chromosomes, ensuring genetic variation in offspring.

questions.text: Which of the following statements about stomata is correct?

options.A: Stomata are pores in the leaf epidermis, controlled by guard cells, and are essential for gaseous exchange.
options.B: Stomata are present only in roots and help in absorption of water.
options.C: Stomata are pores that allow only water vapour to escape but not gases.
options.D: Stomata are pores that remain permanently open for continuous exchange of gases.

questions.explanation: Option A: Correct. Stomata are small pores found in the epidermis of leaves. They are surrounded by two kidney-shaped guard cells that regulate their opening and closing. Stomata are vital for exchange of gases (CO₂ intake for photosynthesis, O₂ release) and also for transpiration (loss of water vapour). Option B: Incorrect. Stomata are found mainly in leaves, not roots. Roots absorb water through root hairs, not stomata. Option C: Incorrect. Stomata allow both gaseous exchange and water vapour loss. They are not restricted to water vapour movement. Option D: Incorrect. Stomata do not remain permanently open. Guard cells regulate their opening and closing depending on environmental conditions (light, water availability, etc.).

questions.text: In some plants, like desert plants, the epidermis has a thick waxy coating of cutin on its outer surface. This adaptation helps the plant to:

options.A: Absorb more water from the soil
options.B: Reduce water loss due to evaporation

questions.explanation: Desert plants live in arid environments with very little water. Their epidermis is coated with cutin, a waxy, waterproof substance. This prevents excessive water loss from the leaves through evaporation, helping the plant survive in dry conditions.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is true for both xylem and phloem?

options.A: Both are complex tissues forming vascular bundles
options.C: Both consist entirely of dead cells
options.D: Both are simple tissues made of one cell type

questions.explanation: Xylem and phloem are complex conducting tissues forming vascular bundles, crucial for plant survival on land. Xylem mainly conducts water and minerals, while phloem conducts food. Both have multiple cell types. Xylem and phloem are examples of such complex tissues. They are both conducting tissues and constitute a vascular bundle. Vascular tissue is a distinctive feature of the complex plants, one that has made possible their survival in the terrestrial environment. Xylem consists of tracheids, vessels, xylem parenchyma and xylem fibres. Tracheids and vessels have thick walls, and many are dead cells when mature. Tracheids and vessels are tubular structures. This allows them to transport water and minerals vertically. The parenchyma stores food. Xylem fibres are mainly supportive in function. Phloem is made up of five types of cells: sieve cells, sieve tubes, companion cells, phloem fibres and the phloem parenchyma. Sieve tubes are tubular cells with perforated walls. Phloem transports food from leaves to other parts of the plant. Except phloem fibres, other phloem cells are living cells.

questions.text: Which of the following correctly describes blood?

options.A: Blood is a solid tissue without any fluid component.
options.B: Blood has a fluid matrix called plasma, in which RBCs, WBCs, and platelets are suspended.
options.C: Blood only transports oxygen and nothing else.
options.D: Blood consists entirely of living cells without any proteins or salts.

questions.explanation: Blood is a connective tissue with a liquid matrix called plasma. This plasma contains proteins, salts, and hormones, and serves as a medium for red blood cells (RBCs), white blood cells (WBCs), and platelets. Blood transports gases (oxygen and carbon dioxide), digested food, hormones, and waste materials to different parts of the body, making it vital for nutrient supply, immunity, and homeostasis.

questions.text: Which of the following statements about bone is correct?

options.A: Bone is a flexible connective tissue mainly used for storing fat.
options.B: Bone is a connective tissue with cells embedded in a hard matrix of calcium and phosphorus, providing support and anchoring muscles.
options.C: Bone has a liquid matrix like blood.
options.D: Bone cells are not embedded in any matrix and are free-floating.

questions.explanation: Bone is a connective tissue with cells embedded in a hard matrix made of calcium and phosphorus compounds. This hard matrix provides structural support to the body and serves as an anchor for muscles, enabling movement. The matrix also stores minerals and is not liquid like blood. Bone is not flexible; it is rigid and strong, providing protection to internal organs and support to the body.

questions.text: Which connective tissue connects muscles to bones and has great strength but limited flexibility?
questions.explanation: Tendons are fibrous connective tissues that transmit muscle contraction force to bones, allowing movement while being strong but not very flexible. Ligaments connect bones to bones. Cartilage provides flexible support. Bone is a rigid connective tissue. Tendons are strong and inelastic, allowing them to effectively transfer the force of muscle contraction to the skeleton.
